name: k8s-security-policies
description: Implement Kubernetes security policies including NetworkPolicy, PodSecurityPolicy, and RBAC for production-grade security. Use when securing Kubernetes clusters, implementing network isolation, or enforcing pod security standards.
capabilities: Generate default-deny NetworkPolicy configurations for namespace isolation
Create RBAC Roles and ClusterRoles with least-privilege permissions
Apply Pod Security Standards labels to namespaces
Configure OPA Gatekeeper ConstraintTemplates for admission control
Set up Istio mTLS and AuthorizationPolicy for service mesh security
Design ServiceAccount configurations with token auto-mount disabled
author: wshobson
securitySummary: This skill contains Kubernetes security documentation and YAML templates. All static findings are false positives: the metadata endpoint IP is shown as an EXAMPLE OF WHAT TO BLOCK (security best practice), backtick patterns are markdown code fence syntax, and 'weak crypto' matches are Kubernetes resource names like 'cluster-admin'. No executable code or malicious patterns present.
valueStatement: Kubernetes clusters need proper security policies to protect against unauthorized access and network attacks. This skill provides ready-to-use templates for NetworkPolicy, RBAC, and Pod Security Standards.
userTitle: Implement Kubernetes Security Policies and RBAC Controls
limitations: Does not deploy policies directly to clusters
Requires CNI plugin that supports NetworkPolicy enforcement
OPA Gatekeeper templates need Gatekeeper installed on cluster

useCases.title: Secure Multi-Tenant Kubernetes Clusters
useCases.description: Implement namespace isolation with NetworkPolicy and RBAC to prevent cross-tenant access in shared clusters.

useCases.title: Achieve Compliance with Security Standards
useCases.description: Apply CIS Kubernetes Benchmark controls using Pod Security Standards and RBAC configurations.

useCases.title: Configure Least-Privilege Access for Services
useCases.description: Create ServiceAccounts and RoleBindings that grant only the permissions your application needs.

promptTemplates.title: Create Default Deny Policy
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a NetworkPolicy that denies all ingress and egress traffic by default for the payments namespace.

promptTemplates.title: Design RBAC for CI/CD
promptTemplates.prompt: Create a ClusterRole and RoleBinding that allows my CI/CD service account to deploy applications but not read secrets.

promptTemplates.title: Configure Pod Security Standards
promptTemplates.scenario: Enforcing container security at namespace level
promptTemplates.prompt: Apply restricted Pod Security Standards to my production namespace with audit logging for violations.

promptTemplates.title: Build Complete Security Policy Set
promptTemplates.prompt: Design a complete security policy set for a microservices application with frontend, backend, and database tiers including NetworkPolicy, RBAC, and Pod Security contexts.

bestPractices: Start with default-deny NetworkPolicy and add specific allow rules
Use namespace-scoped Roles instead of ClusterRoles when possible
Disable ServiceAccount token auto-mounting for pods that do not need API access
antiPatterns: Do not use wildcard verbs or resources in production RBAC rules
Do not skip Pod Security Standards enforcement in production namespaces
Do not grant cluster-admin to application ServiceAccounts

faq.question: Why is my NetworkPolicy not blocking traffic?
faq.answer: Verify your CNI plugin supports NetworkPolicy. Calico, Cilium, and Weave support it. Flannel does not by default.

faq.question: What is the difference between Role and ClusterRole?
faq.answer: Role is namespace-scoped and grants access within one namespace. ClusterRole is cluster-wide and can grant access across all namespaces.

faq.question: Should I use Pod Security Policies or Pod Security Standards?
faq.answer: Use Pod Security Standards. PodSecurityPolicy is deprecated since Kubernetes 1.21 and removed in 1.25.

faq.question: How do I test if RBAC permissions are working?
faq.answer: Use kubectl auth can-i command to test permissions. Example: kubectl auth can-i list pods --as system:serviceaccount:ns:sa

faq.question: What Pod Security Standard level should I use for production?
faq.answer: Use 'restricted' for production workloads. It enforces non-root users, read-only filesystems, and dropped capabilities.

faq.question: How do I allow DNS traffic with a default-deny policy?
faq.answer: Add an egress rule allowing UDP port 53 to the kube-system namespace where CoreDNS runs.
